title: Western Quest Hooks
description: Jobs, troubles, and entanglements from the frontier — ranch disputes, railroad politics, bounties, bank complications, land grabs, and the kind of problems that a town without law eventually exports to people who pass through.

entries.result: A rancher's cattle have been run off his land in the night, and the tracks lead toward a neighboring spread with a lawyer on retainer and friends at the land office.
A man who was hanged six months ago has been spotted in three different towns. Someone wants to know which one is telling the truth.
The railroad surveyors have marked a route through the middle of a town's cemetery. The town has opinions. The railroad has a federal charter.
A bank has been robbed clean — vault, drawers, and the bank manager's personal safe — by a crew that left no tracks, no witnesses, and no trace. The manager hired no one but is hiding something.
A cattle baron has filed a deed claim on water rights to a river that three smaller operations depend on. The deed is legal. The date on it is a forgery.
A federal marshal arrived in town three days ago, took a room, asked some questions, and hasn't been seen since. Nobody's checked his room.
A mine has caved in with two men inside. The mine company says it's unstable and the men are certainly dead. The foreman doesn't believe it.
A bounty is being offered for a man who, by all local accounts, didn't do what the bounty says he did. The man who posted the bounty is not available for comment.
A widow's ranch is profitable and paid off. She's received three separate offers to buy it this month, each from a different buyer with no apparent connection to each other.
A stagecoach arrived carrying the mail, but not the payroll it was also supposed to carry. The driver has a very consistent story. The shotgun guard isn't around to corroborate it.
A town's only doctor has received a threatening letter and is considering leaving. Whoever wants him gone knows something the town hasn't been told.
A land grant from the territorial government has been produced by a company claiming half of the county. The grant looks legitimate. Nobody remembers it being issued.
A rider has arrived carrying a letter from a man who's been dead for two years. The letter is undated, written in the dead man's hand, and describes something that happened last month.
A local rancher's son has been arrested in a distant town on a capital charge. The family can't afford a lawyer. The boy's account of events is internally consistent and completely unbelievable.
Three men arrived in town last week, spent freely, asked no questions, and have been watching the bank every day since.
A Native trading party has come to return something that was stolen from a homestead — but the homestead has no record of owning it.
A mine owner is hiring security and is paying twice the going rate. Nobody's asked why yet. The previous security crew left together and has not been seen.
A woman is passing through town under a false name on her way to testify in a trial about a crime that multiple powerful people would prefer remain untried.
A cattle drive has arrived from the south understaffed, underfed, and short several hundred head of cattle. The trail boss won't explain what happened south of the river.
A surveyor for the railroad has found something during his survey that he won't put in his report but will tell someone he trusts. The railroad has noticed the report is incomplete.
A town's sheriff has deputized himself out of existence — too many deputies, too many favors owed — and now there are eight men with badges and no one enforcing anything.

A man wants to hire someone to find his missing partner. His partner's name, description, and last known location are provided without hesitation. Everything he says is accurate. Something is still wrong.
A family has been farming land for twenty years. A man has arrived with a deed that predates their claim. He hasn't gone to court yet. He's waiting for something.
A horse thief is operating in the county with a method that shouldn't be possible given the geography and timing. Someone with inside knowledge is involved.
A warrant arrives for a local man — well-liked, settled, a neighbor — for crimes committed under a different name in a different state fifteen years ago.
A telegraph message arrived at the station addressed to a man who died before it was sent. The message was from him.
A town is flooding every spring due to a dam built upstream by a cattle company. The company has a permit. The dam is not in the permit.
A gambler has won the deed to a silver claim in a card game and wants it verified before the man he won it from sobers up and changes his mind.
A woman is hiring a crew to recover cattle rustled from her husband's estate — her husband being recently deceased in circumstances that the estate's foreman is very careful about describing.
A string of fires has hit homesteads in the same valley. All small operations, all sold quickly after, all to the same buyer.
A prisoner being transferred is more than the warrant describes, and the men bringing him in are less than they claim.
A silver shipment went missing on a route that only four people knew. All four people have alibis. The silver is gone.
A mining claim has been jumped by a company with a lawyer and a surveyor who arrived the same day. The original claimant's paperwork is in order. So is the company's.
A town wants to incorporate, which would give it self-governance. A territorial politician wants to prevent this. The first step to incorporation requires a census.
A courier carrying papers for a land deal has vanished between two towns four days apart. A search of the route has found his horse but not him.
An old man has died and left his land to people in his community equally — a will that is valid, legal, and that three creditors and one relative are trying to contest before it's filed.
A rancher's fence line is being cut every few nights. He's arrested two men. Neither is responsible. Whoever is responsible is patient.
A traveling judge is coming to settle a land dispute that has been festering for six years. Both sides have had time to prepare. Both sides have done things to prepare that should not be found.
A gun for hire has arrived in town. Nobody knows who hired her. She hasn't made a move. Nobody wants to be the one who sets her off by asking.
A town's water supply has been poisoned — cattle are dying, people are sick — and a man is claiming responsibility on behalf of a dispute the town didn't know it had.
A man wants passage for himself and four companions out of the territory without going through the three towns on the normal routes. He's paying. He's not explaining.
A military patrol is searching for three men and a wagon that passed through town three days ago. The men seemed ordinary. The wagon was covered.
A mine assayer has been producing fraudulent reports for two years. He's decided to come clean. He's hired the party to be present when he does, because he expects a reaction.
A town is preparing for a land auction. Someone has information that the auction is rigged. Getting that information in front of the right people before the auction is the problem.
A veteran lawman is retiring and wants to hand his town over to someone he trusts before the wrong people move in to fill the gap. The selection process is informal.
A drought has hit the valley and the only operational well belongs to a man who is rationing access in exchange for favors and signed agreements.
A ranch has been abandoned suddenly — livestock penned, tools in the barn, food on the table — with no word from the family and no sign of struggle.
A territorial governor's representative has arrived to investigate corruption. The corruption has had time to prepare. The representative is alone.
A town meeting is being called to vote on hiring a sheriff. Two factions want different candidates. A third man — nobody's choice — is watching with interest.
A rancher has been running cattle on open range that he doesn't own and a company has just fenced. The company owns the range. The rancher has nowhere else to go.
A man in custody keeps requesting to speak specifically to someone passing through. He says it's about a matter of significant public interest. He won't say more until the meeting happens.
A map exists that would resolve a decade-old boundary dispute worth significant land and water. The last known owner of the map is dead. The map is somewhere in his effects.
A merchant has been robbed repeatedly on the same route and has started offering more and more for escorts, because every escort has also been robbed.
A family wants to know what happened to their son who left for the territory five years ago with money and ambition and stopped writing two years ago.
A sheriff has been elected who the previous sheriff's allies didn't want. The new sheriff is in town. He has not survived two days in his previous positions.
A man has been accused of murdering his business partner, who was killed with a gun the accused man doesn't own, in a location the accused man can't have reached by the time of death.
A ranching operation is hiring guns openly, publicly, with no stated purpose, and the other operations in the valley are doing the same.
A mine has produced a mineral that isn't in any assayer's reference books. The find is worth a great deal. The claimant has told one too many people.
A man has ridden in from three days south with a serious wound and a specific message for a specific person. The wound is going to kill him. He has maybe a day.
The railroad right-of-way goes through a town that voted to reject it. The town's vote has been overturned by the territorial legislature. The town has not been told yet.
A trapper has been coming into town twice a year for fifteen years. He didn't come in this spring. His trapping range is a week into the mountains.
A military decommission has left a fort empty. Four different parties want the land. The federal disposition process is slow. The land is not.
A woman who was run out of town three years ago has returned with money and a lawyer and is filing for the return of property that was sold in her absence.
A livestock sickness is moving through the valley. A veterinarian believes it's deliberate. The pattern of infection confirms it. The question is who benefits from which herds dying.
A gold strike has been registered. The claim is legitimate. The claimant has not filed for the protection that would make it secure. He has two days before the information spreads.
A homesteader's wife has gone to the law about her husband's disappearance. The law is one of the people she should have told first.
A train robbery left three people dead and the robbers in possession of something that was not on the manifest. The railroad wants it back quietly.
A deal was made on a handshake six years ago. One party has died. The other party is trying to honor the deal. The dead man's heirs have no knowledge of it and no intention of honoring it.
A gang has been taking a small cut of every business in a town for two years. A business owner has stopped paying. He has asked for help before anyone finds out.
A land company is buying out small claims with lowball offers and the strong implication that the alternative is worse. Three families have taken the offer. One family has not.
A former lawman turned criminal turned something else has information about a case that was never solved and is willing to exchange it for help with a problem he can't solve himself.
A dispute about a property line has gone to guns twice and killed two men. A third confrontation is coming. The line dispute itself is based on a survey error from twenty years ago.
A bank examiner has arrived to audit a territorial bank. The bank's owner is not concerned. He should be. The examiner has been paid to find specific things.
A man wants to retire his gun and needs to deliver one last message to a person who does not want to receive it.
A freight company is folding and its contracts are up for bid. One of its routes passes through country that the territory officially considers open but that is not open.
An orphan child found at a burned homestead has a locket with an address and a name in it. The address is three states away.
A former Confederate officer has bought a spread in the territory and his neighbors are not unanimous about this. A federal inquiry is not far behind.
A supply merchant is being squeezed out by a company that's selling at cost in order to drive him out, intending to raise prices after he's gone.
A judge has been bribed to rule against a homestead family. The bribe is documented. Getting the documentation to the appellate court requires someone the briber can't buy.
A man with a grudge has spent three years planning a legal revenge — methodical, patient — and needs one more piece of evidence to make his case.
A town wants to build a school. The land is owned by someone who won't sell but might trade. The trade requires something the town doesn't know how to get.
A night hawk has been running livestock across the border in a pattern that suggests inside knowledge of the patrol schedule.
A murdered man's last action was to post a letter. It hasn't been delivered. The postmaster held it because the addressee is the prime suspect.
A spread that's been vacant for a decade suddenly has riders on it, cattle on it, and a brand that's never been registered.
A cavalry officer has gone AWOL with sensitive dispatches. He's been spotted twice in civilian clothes. His commanding officer wants him back. His commanding officer is also why he ran.
A woman with three children wants to cross to the next territory without going through the normal route and will explain why to someone she trusts, not before.
A man died with a will that gives his considerable estate to a person that his family has never heard of, in a city they've never been to.
A hired killer has arrived in the county and hasn't moved toward any target. He's just present. This is more unsettling than if he'd arrived shooting.
A stage line's contract specifies a route. The route goes through territory that has recently become too dangerous for the contracted rate. The stage line needs a new rate, or a new route, or both.
A man has been falsely identified in a published wanted circular and has been unable to prove otherwise because the authorities who could clear him are the ones who issued the circular.
A rancher's hired hands have collectively quit, citing conditions. The rancher is publicly dismissive. The hands are not exaggerating.
A man wants to die with a clear conscience and needs someone to deliver restitution to three people he wronged, in order, before sundown.
A territorial assembly vote is going to determine the county seat. Both candidate towns have hired people to ensure the outcome. It's not clear which town hired whom.
A family of settlers is broke, their claim is valid, and they've been on the land for two seasons. A company has filed a competing claim. The process favors the company. The land favors the family.
A man has arrived claiming to be the long-lost heir to a significant local estate. His documentation is perfect. His story is too detailed. The family's lawyer is very interested.
A local election is being stolen. Evidence of it exists, is physical, and is currently in the possession of the man doing the stealing.
A train is coming in carrying a man that three different local parties want to intercept before he reaches town. None of them know about each other.
A water tower supplying the town has been poisoned. The town has about two days before the public health situation becomes critical. Someone knows who did it and won't say without protection.
A ranch has been losing cattle in a way that shouldn't be possible — no tracks, no carcasses, no sign — for six weeks. The rancher is starting to say things that suggest he has a theory.

A man walks into town with a reputation he didn't choose, a past he can't escape, and a problem that he's decided to face before it catches up to him. He needs one person he can trust and has run out of time to be careful about who that is.
